title: इन्द्र स्तोत्रम् (वैदिक)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री इन्द्र स्तोत्रम् (वैदिक) ॥

इन्द्रं वो विश्वतस्परि हवामहे जनेभ्यः।
अस्माकमस्तु केवलः॥ १॥

इन्द्र सानसिं रयिं सजित्वानं सदासहम्।
वर्षिष्ठमूतये भर॥ २॥

नि येन मुष्टिहत्यया नि वृत्रा रुणद्धमः।
त्वोतासो न्यर्वता॥ ३॥

इन्द्र नो महधन इन्द्र क्षुद्र उत स्तुतो।
वज्रिन् भुवद् रवित्नवत्॥ ४॥

यो रायो ऽवनिर्महान्त्सुपारः सुन्वतः सखा।
तस्मा इन्द्राय गायत॥ ५॥

त्रातारमिन्द्रमवितारमिन्द्रं हवेहवे सुहवं शूरमिन्द्रम्।
हुवे नु शक्रं पुरुहूतमिन्द्रं स्वस्ति नो मघवा धात्विन्द्रः॥ ६॥

अर्वावतो न आ गहि परावतश्च वृत्रहन्।
उग्रश्चित् ते मन्यते सक्षणिं चिद्युध्यामध्यै॥ ७॥

श्रेष्ठं द्रविणं विभागे सहावान् वज्रिवो जय।
त्वां हि सत्यमद्रिवो हवामहे वाजसातये॥ ८॥

विश्वकर्मन् हविषा वावृधानो महान् देवस्तविषीभिर्बभूव।
तं सू इन्द्रं जगतश्चित् कुविद् गाः सं सूर्यमुषसं ततान॥ ९॥

सत्रा ते अनु कृष्टयो विश्वा तुभ्यमिन्द्र ससुचुः।
महांस्त्वदेव पर्वतो गिरिः वपुष्मान्॥ १०॥

॥ इति श्री इन्द्र स्तोत्रम् (वैदिक) सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Indra Stotram (Vedic) ||

Indram Vo Vishvataspari Havamahe Janebhyah |
Asmakam Astu Kevalah || 1 ||

Indra Sanasim Rayim Sajitvanan Sadasaham |
Varshishtham Utaye Bhara || 2 ||

Ni Yena Mushtihatyaya Ni Vritra Runaddhamah |
Tvotaso Nyarvata || 3 ||

Indra No Mahadhana Indra Kshudra Uta Stutah |
Vajrin Bhuvad Ravitnavat || 4 ||

Yo Rayo Avanir Mahant Suparah Sunvatah Sakha |
Tasma Indraya Gayata || 5 ||

Trataram Indram Avitaram Indram Havehave Suhavam Shuram Indram |
Huve Nu Shakram Puruhutam Indram Svasti No Maghava Dhatv Indrah || 6 ||

Arvavato Na A Gahi Paravatash Cha Vritrahan |
Ugrash Chit Te Manyate Sakshanim Chid Yudhyamadhyai || 7 ||

Shreshtham Dravinam Vibhage Sahavan Vajrivo Jaya |
Tvam Hi Satyam Adrivo Havamahe Vajasataye || 8 ||

Vishvakarman Havisha Vavridhano Mahan Devas Tavishirbhir Babhuva |
Tam Su Indram Jagatash Chit Kuvid Gah Sam Suryam Ushasam Tatana || 9 ||

Satra Te Anu Krishtayo Vishva Tubhyam Indra Sasuchuh |
Mahams Tvadeva Parvato Girih Vapushman || 10 ||

|| Iti Shri Indra Stotram (Vedic) Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
हम सब लोगों के लिए सर्वत्र इन्द्र का आह्वान करते हैं — वे केवल हमारे हों।

हे इन्द्र! सदा विजयी, सदा सहने वाला, श्रेष्ठतम धन हमारी रक्षा के लिए लाओ।

जिनके मुष्टि प्रहार से वृत्रासुर रुक गया — वे इन्द्र हमारी सहायता करें।

हे वज्रिन्! बड़े और छोटे दोनों प्रकार के धन के लिए स्तुत हो।

जो महान धन के रक्षक हैं, सोमयाजी के मित्र हैं — उन इन्द्र के लिए गाओ।

रक्षक इन्द्र, सहायक इन्द्र, बार-बार बुलाने में सुगम शूर इन्द्र — मघवान इन्द्र हमारा कल्याण करें।

हे वृत्रहन! निकट से और दूर से हमारे पास आओ।

हे अद्रिव! हम तुम्हें सत्य में अन्न-प्राप्ति के लिए बुलाते हैं।
benefits: Authentic Vedic hymns to Lord Indra from the Rig Veda
Contains the famous 'Trataram Indram' verse for divine protection
Invokes victory, wealth, strength and divine favor
Most powerful Vedic invocation for overcoming obstacles
Sacred mantras recited in Vedic yajnas and rituals
description: Dedicated to Lord Indra, the Indra Stotram (Vedic) (इन्द्र स्तोत्रम् (वैदिक)) is a devotional hymn of praise revered in Hindu tradition. Attributed to Rig Veda, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Regular recitation is believed to grant victory, wealth, strength and divine favor, authentic Vedic hymns to Lord Indra from the Rig Veda, and contain the famous 'Trataram Indram' verse for divine protection. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Thursday (Guruvar). Comprising 10 verses, it can be completed in approximately 5 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Indra Stotram (Vedic) in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Indra Stotram Vedic in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Authentic Rig Veda hymns to Devaraj Indra for victory and protection.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Indra Stotram (Vedic)?
seoFaqs.answer: The Indra Stotram (Vedic) is a ten-verse compilation of authentic Rig Vedic hymns dedicated to Indra, the king of the devas (Devaraja), wielder of the vajra (thunderbolt), and slayer of the demon Vritra. The verses are drawn from various suktas of the Rig Veda — Indra is the most invoked deity in the Rig Veda, with nearly a quarter of all Rig Vedic hymns addressed to him. The compilation includes the celebrated 'Trataram Indram avitaram Indram' verse (Rig Veda 6.47.11), regarded as one of the most powerful Vedic invocations for divine protection.

seoFaqs.question: Who composed the Vedic Indra hymns?
seoFaqs.answer: The Indra hymns of the Rig Veda are attributed to many seer-families (rishi-kulas) — including Madhuchchhandas Vaishvamitra, Grtsamada Bhargava Shaunaka, Vishvamitra, Vamadeva Gautama, Atri, Bharadvaja, Vasishtha, Kanva, and others. They are not single human compositions but mantras 'seen' by the rishis in deep meditation and transmitted through Vedic oral tradition. Each sukta in the compilation here belongs to one of these rishi families; the compilation as a stotram for daily recitation has been preserved in classical Vedic anthologies.

seoFaqs.question: What are the benefits of reciting Indra Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Recitation of the Vedic Indra Stotram is traditionally said to invoke victory in battles and contests, protection from enemies and obstacles, wealth and prosperity (rayi), strength (bala), and divine favor. Indra is also the rain-giver and lord of the sky, so the stotra is chanted for timely rains, agricultural abundance, and protection from natural calamities. The 'Trataram Indram' verse in particular is regarded as a potent kavacha (shield mantra) for warriors, soldiers, and those facing adversity.

seoFaqs.question: When should Indra Stotram be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Vedic tradition recommends recitation on Thursday (Guruvar), which is sacred to Indra's preceptor Brihaspati, and on Indra-related festivals such as Indra Yatra (in Nepal) and Indra Dhwaja festivals. It is also chanted at Vedic yajnas, especially Soma sacrifices where Indra is the chief recipient of soma. Daily morning recitation after bath, facing east, is considered auspicious for victory and protection. Warriors, athletes, and those in competitive professions traditionally invoke Indra before important events.

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of 'Trataram Indram'?
seoFaqs.answer: 'Trataram Indram avitaram Indram, havehave suhavam shuram Indram, huve nu shakram puruhutam Indram, svasti no maghava dhatv Indrah' translates as 'I invoke Indra the protector, Indra the helper, Indra the easily-invoked hero in every call, I invoke now the mighty Indra praised by many — may bountiful Indra grant us welfare.' This single verse from Rig Veda 6.47.11 is regarded as a complete protection mantra by itself and is often chanted independently as a kavacha.

seoFaqs.question: Who is Indra in Vedic tradition?
seoFaqs.answer: Indra is the king of the devas (Devaraja), lord of Svarga (heaven), wielder of the vajra (thunderbolt), and the foremost warrior-deity of the Rig Veda. He is the slayer of Vritra (the cosmic serpent of drought), releaser of the imprisoned waters and cattle, bringer of rains, and protector of yajna. He rides the elephant Airavata and is married to Shachi (Indrani). Though his prominence diminished in later Puranic Hinduism in favor of Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva, in the Vedic period Indra was the supreme active deity.

seoFaqs.question: How many verses does the Vedic Indra Stotram contain?
seoFaqs.answer: This curated Indra Stotram contains ten verses drawn from various Rig Vedic suktas dedicated to Indra. Each verse stands independently as a complete invocation, and together they cover Indra's principal attributes — protector, victor over Vritra, granter of wealth, lord of strength, friend of the soma-presser, and king of the devas. The full recitation takes approximately five minutes.

seoFaqs.question: What language is the Indra Stotram in?
seoFaqs.answer: The Indra Stotram is in Vedic Sanskrit, the archaic register of Sanskrit used in the Samhitas. It preserves the Vedic accent (svara) — udatta, anudatta, and svarita — which is essential for proper Vedic recitation by trained pandits. On this page the text is presented in Devanagari script with English transliteration and Hindi meaning so that devotees who do not read Vedic accent marks can still chant it with reverence.

seoFaqs.question: Is Indra still worshipped today?
seoFaqs.answer: While Indra is no longer the central deity of household worship in mainstream Hinduism (that role having shifted to the Trimurti and the Goddess), he remains a respected Lokapala (guardian of the eastern direction), is invoked in every Vedic yajna, is honored in Sandhya Vandana, and is celebrated in regional festivals like Indra Yatra in Kathmandu, Indra Dhwaja worship in some farming communities, and in temple consecrations. Vedic pandits continue to chant Indra suktas as part of daily Brahmayajna and Vedic study.
